int minimumOperationsToMakeEqual(int x, int y) {

}

solution: 
```cpp
#include <queue>
#include <unordered_set>
#include <vector>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int minimumOperationsToMakeEqual(int x, int y) {
        if (x == y) return 0;
        
        queue<pair<int, int>> q;
        unordered_set<int> visited;
        
        q.push({x, 0});
        visited.insert(x);
        
        while (!q.empty()) {
            auto [current, steps] = q.front();
            q.pop();
            
            vector<int> nexts;
            nexts.push_back(current + 1);
            nexts.push_back(current - 1);
            
            if (current % 5 == 0) {
                nexts.push_back(current / 5);
            }
            if (current % 11 == 0) {
                nexts.push_back(current / 11);
            }
            
            for (int next : nexts) {
                if (next == y) {
                    return steps + 1;
                }
                if (visited.find(next) == visited.end()) {
                    visited.insert(next);
                    q.push({next, steps + 1});
                }
            }
        }
        
        return -1; // This line is theoretically unreachable
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N), where N is the number of unique states explored.
space complexity: O(N), for storing visited states and queue.
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution uses BFS to explore the shortest path from x to y, considering all possible operations. Each state is processed once, ensuring efficiency. The approach ensures the minimum number of operations is found quickly.
